Member-only story

8 min read
2 hours ago

--

Difficulty: Hard
Topic: Strongly Connected Components, Graph Theory
Problem URL: Facebook Hacker Cup
Problem Summary
You have a directed graph representing a city’s street network. An SCC (Strongly Connected Component) is a maximal set of nodes where every node can reach every other node following directed edges. In other words, it’s a subgraph where you can travel from any node to any other node by following the directed edges.
Gentrification involves removing edges to break SCCs into specific, smaller sizes. The goal is to fragment overcrowded SCCs (neighborhoods that are too connected) into smaller, more manageable communities.
Given a target SCC size K, determine the minimum number of edges you must remove from the graph so that all remaining SCCs have size at most K. This is a complex optimization problem that combines graph decomposition with edge-removal strategies.
The challenge is identifying which edges, when removed, optimally fragment SCCs without over-removing. For example, if a city has one large SCC of 10 neighborhoods and you want SCCs of size at most 3, you need to strategically cut edges to break the SCC into multiple pieces, each of size ≤ 3.
Worked Example
